title: 7 Best Etsy Alternatives in 2025 for Selling Handmade & Vintage
metaDescription: Etsy fees adding up? We compared 7 Etsy alternatives in 2025 for handmade, vintage, and unique goods—with lower fees and more flexible selling.
heroImageAlt: A flat lay of handmade crafts including ceramics, candles, and jewelry arranged artfully with kraft paper tags and a vintage camera
content:
7 Best Etsy Alternatives in 2025 for Selling Handmade & Vintage

Etsy started as a scrappy marketplace for independent makers and vintage hunters. In 2025, it's a sprawling platform where dropshippers and mass producers crowd out the handmade sellers it was built for — and where fees have crept up to the point where a $6.5% transaction fee plus a $0.20 listing fee plus payment processing can quietly devour a meaningful portion of your margins. If you're wondering whether there are better options, there are. Seven of them, in fact.

Etsy vs. 7 Alternatives: The Comparison

Platform Fees Currency Local Pickup Ships Privacy Verdict
Swapedly No fees Swap Bucks (1 SB = $1) Yes Yes No social login required 🏆 Best for Versatility
eBay 13.25% Cash / card No Yes Account required Best for vintage/rare
Amazon Handmade 15% referral Cash / card No Yes Account required High reach, high fee
Facebook Marketplace 5% shipped / free local Cash / card Yes Yes FB profile required Good local option
Mercari No fees Cash / card Yes Yes Account required Easy selling
Bonanza 3.5-25% Cash / card No Yes Account required Low fees, low traffic
Redbubble Print-on-demand margin Cash / card No Yes Account required Best for digital/print art

#1 Swapedly — The Etsy Alternative That Works Beyond Handmade

Here's an honest take on Swapedly vs. Etsy: if you're a handmade seller who relies on Etsy's built-in audience of craft-curious buyers, Swapedly won't replicate that exact demand. Etsy has years of SEO equity and buyer intent baked in for the handmade niche. What Swapedly offers instead is a far more flexible platform with a fee structure that rewards every type of seller.

The Swapedly charges no transaction fees. A $300 vintage find? You pay $20 max. On Etsy, that same $300 item costs you $19.50 in transaction fees plus $0.20 listing fee — comparable, but without a cap that benefits higher-priced items. Sell a $500 vintage piece? Etsy takes $32.50+; Swapedly takes $20.

The Swap Bucks (SB) currency is what makes the Swapedly ecosystem genuinely interesting for makers and vintage sellers. Sell your handmade goods, earn SB, and use those SB to buy supplies, vintage pieces to upcycle, or other items for your shop or home. 1 SB = $1 in platform value. It's a way to reinvest in your craft without touching your bank account.

New users get 40 Swap Bucks free — that's immediate purchasing power to source your next vintage find or restock supplies. Claim yours at swapedly.com. The Swapedly Plus tier at $9.99/month amplifies your SB earnings by 1.5x, which adds up meaningfully for regular sellers.

Another key advantage: Swapedly supports both local pickup and shipping. Etsy is purely online-shipped. If you sell at craft fairs or have local regulars, Swapedly lets you capture that local demand too — no Etsy listing fees on items that never ship.

Swapedly Pros

  • Swapedly charges no transaction fees. Vintage cameras, antique furniture, rare glassware, obscure records: eBay buyers come looking and come ready to pay.

    The 13.25% fee is higher than Etsy's 6.5%, which is a real cost. But the buyer pool difference on vintage items can more than compensate. Run the numbers for your specific items — for some categories, eBay's audience justifies the fee premium.

    eBay Pros

    • Massive global reach for rare and vintage items
    • Auction format can drive prices above Etsy listings
    • Strong collector communities

    eBay Cons

    • 13.25% fee exceeds Etsy
    • No handmade community or creative focus
    • Complex, competitive environment

    #3 Amazon Handmade — High Reach, High Cost

    Amazon Handmade reaches more eyeballs than almost any other platform on earth, and for handcrafted goods that can compete on Amazon's search results, that exposure is powerful. The application process is selective — Amazon actually reviews applications for handmade status — which theoretically keeps quality higher than Etsy.

    The 15% referral fee is steeper than Etsy's 6.5% — both higher than Swapedly's zero transaction fee, and the Amazon environment is relentlessly price-competitive. You're also at the mercy of Amazon's policies and the looming threat that Amazon might create a competing product if yours sells well enough. It happens.

    Amazon Handmade Pros

    • Enormous traffic and buyer trust
    • Application screening keeps quality higher
    • FBA available for fulfillment

    Amazon Handmade Cons

    • 15% referral fee — highest on this list
    • Brutal price competition
    • Amazon's brand dominates, not yours

    #4 Facebook Marketplace — The Local Craft Fair Alternative

    For vintage furniture, large handmade pieces, or anything where local buyers who want to inspect before purchasing would be a natural audience, Facebook Marketplace is a free tool worth using. No listing fees, no transaction fees for local cash sales, and the reach in most areas is genuinely solid.

    For a seller who does both local craft events and online sales, Facebook Marketplace complements Swapedly well: use Swapedly for shipped items and Swap Bucks; use Facebook for large local pieces where shipping would be impractical.

    Facebook Marketplace Pros

    • Free for local transactions
    • Good for large items that can't ship easily
    • Large local audience

    Facebook Marketplace Cons

    • Your Facebook identity is fully visible
    • No handmade/vintage audience focus
    • Cash-only local sales offer no protection

    #5 Mercari — The No-Fuss Etsy Alternative

    Mercari's no transaction fee with no listing charge is cleaner than Etsy's structure if you're selling items individually rather than building a storefront. No monthly listing fees, no $0.20 charges per item — you list it, it sells, you pay 10%. For smaller handmade or vintage items where Etsy's listing fee represents a significant percentage of margin, Mercari makes sense.

    Mercari Pros

    • No listing fees — just 10% on sale
    • Simple, fast listing process
    • Accepts all categories including handmade

    Mercari Cons

    • No craft/vintage community to drive organic traffic
    • No fee cap — 10% regardless of price
    • Buyer service fee complicates pricing

    #6 Bonanza — Low Fees, Niche Audience

    Bonanza is a small but dedicated marketplace that positions itself as the seller-friendly Etsy alternative. Fees start at 3.5% for low-priced items, making it potentially the cheapest option here for budget-priced goods. The platform imports listings from eBay and Etsy easily, so cross-listing is painless.

    The limitation is traffic. Bonanza has a small buyer pool compared to Etsy or eBay, and organic discoverability is limited. Sellers who succeed there typically drive their own traffic through social media or email lists rather than relying on Bonanza's internal search.

    Bonanza Pros

    • Fees as low as 3.5% — lowest on this list
    • Easy cross-listing from Etsy/eBay
    • Seller-friendly policies

    Bonanza Cons

    • Very low buyer traffic — you must drive your own
    • Limited brand awareness
    • Fee scales up to 25% for advertising-boosted sales

    #7 Redbubble — Best for Digital and Print Artists

    Redbubble is for a specific type of maker: artists who create digital designs that can be printed on shirts, mugs, phone cases, and other products. You upload your art, Redbubble handles production, inventory, and shipping. Your margin is the markup above their base price.

    For craft-based makers who produce physical goods, Redbubble isn't relevant. But for illustrators, graphic designers, and print artists, it removes the entire production and fulfillment burden. The tradeoff is that your "store" is really just a Redbubble page, not a brand you control.

    Redbubble Pros

    • Zero inventory or fulfillment required
    • Great for digital designers and artists
    • Passive income potential once designs are uploaded

    Redbubble Cons

    • Only relevant for digital/print art — not physical handmade goods
    • Thin margins — you compete on Redbubble's platform, not your own
    • Low brand ownership

    Which Etsy Alternative Is Right for Your Shop?

    If you've built an Etsy shop with reviews and followers, don't blow it up to switch platforms. But for new sellers — or those who want to diversify — the calculus is clear. Swapedly offers the most flexibility: all categories, local and shipped options, a fee cap that rewards higher-priced items, and $40 free to start. Etsy's built-in handmade audience is real but increasingly crowded and hard to break into.

    For vintage collectors who sell expensive one-of-a-kind pieces, eBay's global reach is worth the fee premium. For pure digital artists, Redbubble handles everything. For makers who just want the simplest possible fee structure with no listing charges, Swapedly or Mercari are the right calls.

    See also our guide to the best Amazon Marketplace alternatives and our full ranking of the best places to sell stuff online in 2025.
